extracted.brandBackground: Sola Salons licenses turn-key salon studios to independent beauty professionals.

leadership.name: Ben Jones
leadership.background: Served as Chief Legal Counsel and Chief Development Officer of Sola and affiliates.
leadership.whyItMatters: Provides deep legal and development continuity for the brand.

leadership.name: Robert Michael Bell Jr.
leadership.background: Previously served as President and CFO of WellBiz Brands, Inc.
leadership.whyItMatters: Brings extensive franchise financial leadership experience.

leadership.name: B. Keith Sizemore
leadership.background: Previously served as Senior VP of Development for Shipley Donuts.
leadership.whyItMatters: Brings strong multi-unit development and operations experience.

leadership.name: Daryl Hurst
leadership.background: Previously served as President and COO for Maaco Paint & Autobody.
leadership.whyItMatters: Brings extensive franchise operations and brand management experience.

cohorts.label: Sola Salons Franchised Locations Occupancy
cohorts.basis: Occupancy percentage of 653 franchised locations open at least 13 months as of December 31, 2025.

cohorts.label: Sola Salons Franchised Locations Gross Revenue
cohorts.basis: Gross revenue of 653 franchised locations open at least 13 months as of December 31, 2025.

cohorts.label: Sola Salons Company-Owned Locations Performance
cohorts.basis: Average performance of 62 company-owned locations open at least 18 months as of December 31, 2025.

cohorts.label: Sola Salons Company-Owned Locations EBITDA
cohorts.basis: Average estimated earnings after operating expenses of 62 company-owned locations open at least 18 months as of December 31, 2025.

item19.notes: Item 19 provides occupancy data, gross revenue data for franchised locations, and detailed P&L data for company-owned locations.

lineItems.notes: Paid upon signing the Franchise Agreement.
Paid to direct a marketing campaign.
Varies by state and local requirements.
Prepayment of first month's rent and security deposit.
For common areas and individual studios.
Hardware and software meeting minimum standards.

hiddenCosts.name: Market Introduction Fee
hiddenCosts.description: Mandatory fee of $20,000 due 60 days after signing lease.

hiddenCosts.name: Technology Fee
hiddenCosts.description: Monthly fee of $185 to $250 per location.

hiddenCosts.name: Property Management Software
hiddenCosts.description: Rent Manager sublicense fee of $99 per month plus $150 setup fee.

operationalRisks.title: Out-of-State Dispute Resolution
operationalRisks.description: Disputes must be resolved in Colorado, which may increase costs.

operationalRisks.title: Spousal Liability
operationalRisks.description: Spouses must sign a personal guarantee, placing personal assets at risk.

operationalRisks.title: Mandatory Minimum Payments
operationalRisks.description: Franchisees must pay a minimum royalty of $500 per month regardless of sales.

extracted.conceptRationale: Sola Salons operates as a turn-key salon studio rental business for beauty professionals.
extracted.staffingRationale: The business model relies on a single manager to oversee multiple independent salon professionals.

riskReasons: Rent is heavy: ~39% of mid-cohort revenue (above 25%).
Long payback: ~5.7 years on the build-out before financing.
Capital gap of $1,099,255 is ~4.4x your liquid — heavy reliance on outside financing.
A high-severity operational tripwire is disclosed — see the tripwires section.

midCohort.label: Sola Salons Franchised Locations Gross Revenue

source.label: Sola Salons Franchised Locations Gross Revenue

underwriting.assessment: With $250,000 liquid against a $1,349,255 mid-point build-out, there is a $1,099,255 capital gap. An SBA loan or an equity partner is required to do this deal. At mid-cohort revenue, modeled cash flow after debt service is about $4,934/mo before payroll and maintenance — a thin margin of error.

insights.conceptRationale: Sola Salons operates as a turn-key salon studio rental business for beauty professionals.

benchmark.label: Beauty / personal care
benchmark.dominantRisk: Commission/booth-rent labor and chair utilization. Labor structure dominates economics.
considerations: Labor (commission or booth-rent) is the big line at ~35–50%; COGS on product is modest.
Verify chair/room utilization and stylist retention with Item 20 operators.
Confirm whether labor runs on commission, hourly, or booth rent — it changes the model entirely.

crossCheck.message: No operating-EBITDA margin is disclosed in Item 19. The 8–18% band below is an industry estimate for this concept — validate it against real franchisee P&Ls (Item 20) before relying on any margin.

insights.staffingNote: Flagged as an lightly staffed model from the FDD, so labor is adjusted down to 18–30% — the 35–50% category band assumes a staffed venue. Confirm actual staffing with Item 20 franchisees.

buildup.label: − Labor
buildup.note: lightly staffed · ≈ 1.9–3.2 FTE at ~$20/hr fully loaded

assumptions.detail: Classified as “Beauty / personal care” from the FDD (Item 1 / brand description).
Read as lightly staffed from the FDD, which lowers the labor band applied below.
Taken from the FDD (~$14,604/mo); already netted in margin after fees & rent.
Estimated at the 8–18% category band — COGS is never disclosed in an FDD.
Estimated at the 18–30% lightly staffed band — labor is never disclosed in an FDD.
Utilities, insurance, and R&M estimated at 6–10% (category catch-all).
Our calculation — the disclosed top line minus the benchmarked costs above. Not a figure stated in the FDD.

insights.consultCtaPitch: These are category benchmarks applied to the disclosed top line — your unit's real costs will differ, and that difference is the conversation. Want them pressure-tested against actual franchisee P&Ls for your territory?
insights.asOf: 2026 — general industry benchmarks, refine against real franchisee P&Ls
insights.disclaimer: Industry benchmark ranges for your own budgeting and Item 20 questions — NOT a projection of this franchise's results, and not investment advice.

financialCondition.headline: The franchisor's statements don't show the distress signals we screen for — positive net worth and a clean audit. Still worth a quick confirmation against current figures.
body: Why it matters to you: field support, training, technology, and marketing come out of the franchisor's budget. If outside funding slows, those are the first things to get cut. Radiance Intermediate, LLC guarantees the franchisor's performance, which partly backstops this.

Verify before you commit: ask for the most recent interim (unaudited) financials and the current-year funding plan; ask whether the parent will sign a guarantee of performance; and cross-check Item 20 — unit closures, transfers, and terminations are the operational tell that corroborates financial stress.
mitigants: the auditor issued a clean (unmodified) opinion and did not raise going-concern doubt
the parent guarantees the franchisor's performance
financialCondition.evidenceNote: Derived from the Item 21 / Exhibit F audited financials. Figures are extracted; all ratios and grading are computed. Confirm against the source statements and a CPA before relying on them.
